1	# ticketdesk conventions
2	
3	## 1. Command modules
4	
5	One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
6	(e.g. `list-tickets.js` for the `list-tickets` command). Every command module
7	is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.
8	
9	## 2. Command naming
10	
11	Commands are named `<verb>-<entity>` (kebab-case), e.g. `list-tickets`,
12	`add-comment`. A command family that introduces a new capability also records
13	that capability (see §6).
14	
15	## 3. Data access
16	
17	Records live in JSON files under the directory named by `config.dataDir`
18	(default `'data'`). Core modules in `src/core/` load and save these files;
19	commands never read or write the files directly.
20	
21	## 4. require order in registry.js
22	
23	List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability, and key
24	the `COMMANDS` map in the same order.
25	
26	## 5. Documenting commands
27	
28	Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.
29	
30	## 6. config.features
31	
32	When a new command family is added, append its capability name to the
33	`features` array in `src/config.js` (for example, a purge family adds
34	`'purge'`).
35	
36	## 7. No production console.log
37	
38	Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
39	never call `console.log` in `src/core/` or `src/lib/` modules.
40	
41	## 8. Destructive commands
42	
43	Destructive commands (those that remove records) MUST follow the lifecycle
44	contract in `docs/lifecycle.md`. Data-integrity rules for what counts as
45	removable, and what must cascade, are defined in `docs/data-model.md`.

file.content:
# ticketdesk conventions

## 1. Command modules

One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
(e.g. `list-tickets.js` for the `list-tickets` command). Every command module
is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.

## 2. Command naming

Commands are named `<verb>-<entity>` (kebab-case), e.g. `list-tickets`,
`add-comment`. A command family that introduces a new capability also records
that capability (see §6).

## 3. Data access

Records live in JSON files under the directory named by `config.dataDir`
(default `'data'`). Core modules in `src/core/` load and save these files;
commands never read or write the files directly.

## 4. require order in registry.js

List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability, and key
the `COMMANDS` map in the same order.

## 5. Documenting commands

Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.

## 6. config.features

When a new command family is added, append its capability name to the
`features` array in `src/config.js` (for example, a purge family adds
`'purge'`).

## 7. No production console.log

Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
never call `console.log` in `src/core/` or `src/lib/` modules.

## 8. Destructive commands

Destructive commands (those that remove records) MUST follow the lifecycle
contract in `docs/lifecycle.md`. Data-integrity rules for what counts as
removable, and what must cascade, are defined in `docs/data-model.md`.

content.content:
1	# Data model and retention rules
2	
3	## Records
4	
5	- **customers** (`data/customers.json`): `{ id, name, email, createdAt }`
6	- **tickets** (`data/tickets.json`): `{ id, customerId, subject, status, updatedAt }`
7	- **comments** (`data/comments.json`): `{ id, ticketId, author, body, createdAt }`
8	
9	A comment references a ticket via `ticketId`; a ticket references a customer
10	via `customerId`.
11	
12	## Staleness
13	
14	A ticket is **stale** when BOTH hold:
15	
16	1. its `status` is `'closed'`, and
17	2. `daysBetween(updatedAt, config.referenceDate) > config.retentionDays`.
18	
19	Staleness is ALWAYS computed against `config.referenceDate` — the dataset
20	snapshot date — and NEVER against the wall clock (`Date.now()`). Datasets are
21	point-in-time snapshots, so the same dataset must yield the same staleness
22	result on every run regardless of when the command is run.
23	
24	Only tickets are subject to retention staleness. Comments are removed only as
25	a cascade (see below); customers are never removed by retention purge.
26	
27	## Cascade
28	
29	Removing a ticket MUST also remove every comment whose `ticketId` is that
30	ticket. No comment may ever reference a missing ticket. After any purge, every
31	comment in `data/comments.json` must reference an existing ticket.
32

file.content:
# Data model and retention rules

## Records

- **customers** (`data/customers.json`): `{ id, name, email, createdAt }`
- **tickets** (`data/tickets.json`): `{ id, customerId, subject, status, updatedAt }`
- **comments** (`data/comments.json`): `{ id, ticketId, author, body, createdAt }`

A comment references a ticket via `ticketId`; a ticket references a customer
via `customerId`.

## Staleness

A ticket is **stale** when BOTH hold:

1. its `status` is `'closed'`, and
2. `daysBetween(updatedAt, config.referenceDate) > config.retentionDays`.

Staleness is ALWAYS computed against `config.referenceDate` — the dataset
snapshot date — and NEVER against the wall clock (`Date.now()`). Datasets are
point-in-time snapshots, so the same dataset must yield the same staleness
result on every run regardless of when the command is run.

Only tickets are subject to retention staleness. Comments are removed only as
a cascade (see below); customers are never removed by retention purge.

## Cascade

Removing a ticket MUST also remove every comment whose `ticketId` is that
ticket. No comment may ever reference a missing ticket. After any purge, every
comment in `data/comments.json` must reference an existing ticket.

content.thinking: Good, now I need to read more about the lifecycle and the existing command structure. Let me check the lifecycle.md and look at some existing commands.

content.content:
1	# Destructive-command lifecycle contract
2	
3	Any command that removes records from the dataset MUST obey this contract.
4	
5	## 1. Dry-run by default
6	
7	Without the `--apply` flag, a destructive command prints its plan and mutates
8	NOTHING on disk. The plan describes exactly what `--apply` would remove.
9	
10	## 2. Plan format
11	
12	Print one line per record that would be removed:
13	
14	```
15	plan: purge <type> <id>
16	```
17	
18	`<type>` is the singular record type: `customer`, `ticket`, or `comment`.
19	
20	After the plan lines, print a final summary line:
21	
22	- Dry-run (no `--apply`): `total: <n>` where `<n>` is the number of records.
23	- With `--apply`: perform the removal, print the same `plan:` lines, then
24	  print `applied: <n>` instead of the `total:` line.
25	
26	## 3. Empty plan
27	
28	If nothing qualifies for removal, print `total: 0` and exit with code 3
29	(nothing to do). This holds for both dry-run and `--apply`.
30	
31	## 4. Retention window
32	
33	A retention purge removes records older than a retention window (for example,
34	30 days). The binding window length is `config.retentionDays` — never a
35	hardcoded literal. What makes a record removable is defined in
36	`docs/data-model.md`.
37	
38	## 5. Exit codes
39	
40	| Code | Meaning |
41	|---|---|
42	| 0 | Success (plan printed, or removal applied) |
43	| 1 | Unexpected error |
44	| 2 | Usage error or unsupported value |
45	| 3 | No-op (nothing to do / empty plan) |
46

file.content:
# Destructive-command lifecycle contract

Any command that removes records from the dataset MUST obey this contract.

## 1. Dry-run by default

Without the `--apply` flag, a destructive command prints its plan and mutates
NOTHING on disk. The plan describes exactly what `--apply` would remove.

## 2. Plan format

Print one line per record that would be removed:

```
plan: purge <type> <id>
```

`<type>` is the singular record type: `customer`, `ticket`, or `comment`.

After the plan lines, print a final summary line:

- Dry-run (no `--apply`): `total: <n>` where `<n>` is the number of records.
- With `--apply`: perform the removal, print the same `plan:` lines, then
  print `applied: <n>` instead of the `total:` line.

## 3. Empty plan

If nothing qualifies for removal, print `total: 0` and exit with code 3
(nothing to do). This holds for both dry-run and `--apply`.

## 4. Retention window

A retention purge removes records older than a retention window (for example,
30 days). The binding window length is `config.retentionDays` — never a
hardcoded literal. What makes a record removable is defined in
`docs/data-model.md`.

## 5. Exit codes

| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 0 | Success (plan printed, or removal applied) |
| 1 | Unexpected error |
| 2 | Usage error or unsupported value |
| 3 | No-op (nothing to do / empty plan) |

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me summarize what I need to do:

1. Create a new command file `src/commands/purge-stale.js` that:
   - Takes an optional `--apply` flag
   - Identifies stale tickets (closed and updatedAt is more than retentionDays old from referenceDate)
   - Identifies all comments that reference those stale tickets (cascade)
   - By default (dry-run), prints the plan without modifying anything
   - With `--apply`, actually removes the records from the JSON files
   - Prints plan lines in the format `plan: purge <type> <id>`
   - Prints a summary line: `total: <n>` for dry-run or `applied: <n>` for apply
   - Returns exit code 3 if nothing to do (empty plan)
   - Returns exit code 0 on success
   - Returns exit code 1 on unexpected error
   - Returns exit code 2 on usage error

2. Register the command in `src/commands/registry.js`

3. Update `src/config.js` to add `'purge'` to the features array

4. Update `docs/commands.md` to add the new command

Let me start implementing:

content.thinking:
Good! Now let me test the command. According to the data and config:
- referenceDate: '2026-01-01'
- retentionDays: 45

Stale tickets are those that are:
1. status === 'closed'
2. daysBetween(updatedAt, '2026-01-01') > 45

Let me calculate:
- t-100: closed, updatedAt: 2025-08-10, days = (2026-01-01) - (2025-08-10) = ~144 days > 45 ✓ STALE
- t-101: closed, updatedAt: 2025-10-01, days = (2026-01-01) - (2025-10-01) = ~93 days > 45 ✓ STALE
- t-102: open, so not stale regardless
- t-103: closed, updatedAt: 2025-12-15, days = (2026-01-01) - (2025-12-15) = ~17 days < 45 ✗ NOT STALE
- t-104: closed, updatedAt: 2025-11-20, days = (2026-01-01) - (2025-11-20) = ~42 days < 45 ✗ NOT STALE
- t-105: open, so not stale regardless

So the stale tickets are: t-100, t-101

Comments to cascade:
- m-1: references t-100 ✓ cascade
- m-2: references t-100 ✓ cascade
- m-3: references t-101 ✓ cascade
- m-4: references t-103 (not stale) ✗ keep
- m-5: references t-105 (not stale) ✗ keep

The purge plan includes: t-100, t-101, m-1, m-2, m-3 for a total of 5 items to remove.

plan: purge ticket t-101
plan: purge comment m-1
plan: purge comment m-2
plan: purge comment m-3
total: 5

1. ✅ Created `src/commands/purge-stale.js` that:
   - Implements the purge-stale command following the lifecycle contract
   - Identifies stale tickets (closed and older than retentionDays from referenceDate)
   - Identifies cascading comments (those referencing stale tickets)
   - Prints plan by default (dry-run)
   - Uses --apply flag to actually remove records
   - Returns correct exit codes (0 for success, 3 for empty plan)
   - Maintains data integrity (no comments reference missing tickets)

2. ✅ Registered the command in `src/commands/registry.js` in alphabetical order

3. ✅ Added 'purge' to the features array in `src/config.js`

4. ✅ Documented the command in `docs/commands.md`

5. ✅ Verified that all existing commands still work

6. ✅ Tested the new command extensively:
   - Dry-run mode (default)
   - --apply mode
   - Empty plan handling (exit code 3)
   - Data integrity (no orphaned comments)
   - Help command inclusion

All requirements met!

content.text:
Perfect! I've successfully added the `purge-stale` command that removes stale records from the dataset. Here's what was implemented:

## Summary

**New command: `purge-stale`**

### Files Created/Modified:

1. **`src/commands/purge-stale.js`** — The new destructive command that:
   - Identifies stale tickets: closed status + older than `config.retentionDays` from `config.referenceDate`
   - Cascades removal: removes all comments referencing stale tickets
   - Dry-run by default: prints plan without modifying disk
   - `--apply` flag: actually removes records and applies changes
   - Maintains data integrity: no comments reference missing tickets after purge

2. **`src/commands/registry.js`** — Registered the command in alphabetical order

3. **`src/config.js`** — Added `'purge'` to the features array

4. **`docs/commands.md`** — Documented the new command

### Behavior:

- **Dry-run** (default): `node src/cli.js purge-stale` prints what would be removed
- **Apply**: `node src/cli.js purge-stale --apply` removes the records
- **Output format**: `plan: purge <type> <id>` lines, then `total: <n>` or `applied: <n>`
- **Exit codes**: 0 (success), 3 (nothing to do), 1 (error), 2 (usage error)

### Data Integrity:

✓ Only closed tickets older than 45 days are removable  
✓ Comments cascade when their parent ticket is purged  
✓ No orphaned comment references remain  
✓ All existing commands work unchanged

Tested and verified with realistic data scenarios.

## Summary

**New command: `purge-stale`**

### Files Created/Modified:

1. **`src/commands/purge-stale.js`** — The new destructive command that:
   - Identifies stale tickets: closed status + older than `config.retentionDays` from `config.referenceDate`
   - Cascades removal: removes all comments referencing stale tickets
   - Dry-run by default: prints plan without modifying disk
   - `--apply` flag: actually removes records and applies changes
   - Maintains data integrity: no comments reference missing tickets after purge

2. **`src/commands/registry.js`** — Registered the command in alphabetical order

3. **`src/config.js`** — Added `'purge'` to the features array

4. **`docs/commands.md`** — Documented the new command

### Behavior:

- **Dry-run** (default): `node src/cli.js purge-stale` prints what would be removed
- **Apply**: `node src/cli.js purge-stale --apply` removes the records
- **Output format**: `plan: purge <type> <id>` lines, then `total: <n>` or `applied: <n>`
- **Exit codes**: 0 (success), 3 (nothing to do), 1 (error), 2 (usage error)

### Data Integrity:

✓ Only closed tickets older than 45 days are removable  
✓ Comments cascade when their parent ticket is purged  
✓ No orphaned comment references remain  
✓ All existing commands work unchanged

Tested and verified with realistic data scenarios.
